description: Where residential mortgage lending is material, the Board must take reasonable steps to satisfy itself as to the level of risk and effectiveness of the risk management framework, including addressing it in the risk appetite, risk management strategy and business plans.

The Board should seek assurances from senior management that the approved risk appetite is communicated to relevant persons and reflected in policies and procedures, and that a robust management information system is in place.

Where residential mortgage lending is material, quantifiable risk limits should be set for various aspects of the portfolio including loan types, origination channels, geographic concentrations, serviceability criteria, LVRs, LMI use, special circumstance loans, override frequency and default/arrears rates.
The risk management framework should clearly specify whether risk limits are 'hard' limits requiring immediate escalation or 'soft' limits where temporary breaches are tolerated.

The risk management framework applicable to the residential mortgage lending portfolio should be subject to a comprehensive review by an operationally independent and competent person at least every three years, with results reported to the Board.
A prudent ADI should conduct a periodic assessment of compliance with responsible lending conduct obligations to avoid significant financial loss.

ADIs with material residential mortgage exposure should invest in management information systems that capture risk metrics at the point of application and throughout the life of the loan, enabling portfolio-level and individual loan-level analysis.

Where the residential mortgage lending portfolio is material, a prudent ADI should apply its remuneration policy to persons involved in residential mortgage lending, including remuneration of third parties such as mortgage broker firms responsible for originating a material proportion of the portfolio.

A prudent ADI should have appropriate monitoring and controls to guard against broker commission incentives that encourage loans with inadequate verification, marginal serviceability, excessive leverage or unsuitable terms, including consideration of clawback provisions.

A prudent ADI relying on brokers to originate a material proportion of its residential mortgage portfolio should closely monitor performance at the broker firm or individual broker level, and take measures including restricting or terminating relationships where elevated defaults or deficient documentation are identified.

APRA expects an ADI to undertake a new serviceability assessment whenever there are material changes to the current or originally approved loan conditions, including changes in repayment basis, interest rate type, tenor or any change that increases total repayments over the life of the loan.
Material serviceability policies and procedures should be reviewed at least annually and updated to align with the changing external environment and risk appetite changes.

APRA expects that any material changes to an ADI's serviceability policy would be analysed and the potential impact on the risk profile of new loans reported to appropriate risk governance forums; reference to competitors' policies as the primary justification for changes is indicative of weak risk governance.

Where an ADI uses different serviceability criteria for different products or brands, APRA expects the ADI to be able to articulate the commercial and other reasons for these differences and any implications for its risk profile and risk appetite.

ADIs must apply a serviceability buffer of at least 3.0 percentage points over the loan's interest rate (ignoring introductory/honeymoon rates), as required under Attachment C of APS 220, and should use this buffer in conjunction with an interest rate floor.

APRA expects ADIs to fully apply buffers and floor rates to both a borrower's new and existing debt commitments, making sufficient enquiries on existing debt including current interest rate, remaining term, outstanding balance, redraw availability and delinquency evidence.

APRA expects ADIs to use a suitably prudent period for assessing repayment of credit card or other revolving personal debt, for example assessing repayment obligations at three per cent per month on the total committed limit.

For interest-only loans, APRA expects ADIs to assess the borrower's ability to meet future repayments on a principal and interest basis for the specific term over which principal and interest repayments apply, excluding the interest-only period.

A prudent ADI should regularly review its serviceability buffers and floors, typically on a quarterly basis and when interest rates change, taking into account historical interest rate movements, forecasts and key economic indicators.
A prudent ADI should apply discounts of at least 20 per cent on most types of non-salary income when assessing borrower income for serviceability, with higher discounts appropriate in some cases.
Prudent serviceability policies should incorporate a minimum haircut of 20 per cent on expected rental income, with larger haircuts appropriate for properties with higher risk of non-occupancy.

APRA expects ADIs to use the greater of a borrower's declared living expenses or an appropriately scaled version of the HEM or HPI indices (with a margin linked to the borrower's income), and to update these indices in loan calculators at least in line with published updates (typically quarterly).

A prudent ADI should retain complete documentation of information supporting a residential mortgage approval, including income and expense documentation and verification steps, for a reasonable number of years after origination.

APRA expects an ADI to have a framework that clearly defines overrides, including capturing and reporting any loan approved outside serviceability criteria parameters (including loans with NIS below $0 or waivers on income verification), with risk limits reflecting the maximum level of allowable overrides.

APRA expects that where overrides breach risk limits, appropriate action would be taken by senior management to investigate and address such excesses; regular override reporting should be provided to senior management and the Board.

APRA expects an ADI to have appropriate processes to determine whether loans approved through overrides to internal policy would be treated as standard or non-standard loans for capital purposes under APS 112.
APRA expects an ADI to consider a borrower's HELP debt obligations alongside other debt commitments when assessing borrowing capacity, as HELP repayments are deducted from gross income.

An ADI retains both the risk and responsibility for material outsourced functions including origination and ongoing management of residential mortgage lending; where lending authority is delegated to third parties, robust oversight mechanisms and regular audit of adherence to policies are required.

A prudent ADI should have appropriate procedures to verify the accuracy and completeness of information provided by third-party originators, and should perform income verification itself rather than delegating it to third parties where possible.

Scorecards or models used in residential mortgage loan evaluation should be subject to regular monitoring and validation, with an effective governance framework overseen by senior management; independent third-party validation is prudent where vendor conflict of interest exists.

APRA expects an ADI to recognise in its risk appetite portfolio limits for loans more vulnerable to serviceability stress and property value decreases, including interest-only, foreign currency, non-standard documentation, reverse mortgage, HELOC and SMSF loans.

APRA expects that an ADI would only approve interest-only loans for owner-occupiers where there is a sound and documented economic basis, not based on inability to service on a principal and interest basis, and that interest-only periods would be of limited duration.

It is not prudent to extend foreign currency-denominated loans against domestic currency income streams or domestic currency collateral; where foreign currency loans are extended to borrowers with foreign currency income, offshore income should be discounted in the serviceability assessment.

For non-standard/alternative documentation loans, a prudent ADI should address increased risk through appropriate pricing and significantly lower LVRs, and should not underwrite loans on limited income verification where full verification is reasonably available.

APRA expects that a prudent ADI would identify the additional risks relevant to SMSF lending, implement appropriate loan assessment processes, and that a decision to undertake SMSF lending would be approved at an appropriate governance forum and explicitly incorporated into the ADI's policy framework.
APRA expects that an ADI will document its valuation policy; as collateral risk increases or loan coverage by collateral decreases, the need for specialist valuation increases.
Valuer selection should be conducted by the ADI's risk management area rather than sales staff, and valuation panels should be periodically reviewed by senior risk management staff.

Sound risk management practices require valuation reports prepared with professional skill and diligence, valuers selected on the basis of appropriate professional qualifications, and comprehensive valuation documentation maintained for the term of the loan.

When valuation management is outsourced, oversight and control should be retained by the ADI's credit risk management, and any override of valuation requirements should be limited to senior credit risk management.

LVRs above 90 per cent (including capitalised LMI premium or other fees) clearly expose an ADI to higher risk of loss; significant increases in high LVR lending should trigger senior management review of risk targets and internal controls with Board oversight.
LVRs should be recalculated at the time of any top-up loan, formal loan increase, refinancing or significant draw-down on equity, based on appropriate and contemporary property valuations.

APRA does not consider the sole use of LMI coverage as a sufficient control to mitigate high LVR risk; where higher LVR lending is permitted, additional mitigants such as stronger serviceability-adjusted pricing and higher expected loss provisions and capital (for IRB banks) should be applied.

For off-the-plan sales, a prudent ADI should make appropriate reductions to developer prices when determining LVRs or seek independent professional valuations, and developer discounts should not be treated as part of the borrower's deposit.

A prudent ADI should assess the guarantor's income and conduct independent checks on creditworthiness, enforceability of claims and value of collateral pledged by the guarantor, and consider appropriate portfolio limits for guarantor loans.
APRA expects that an ADI, as part of its credit risk appetite framework, will define its approach to resolving troubled loans both individually and under conditions of widespread borrower distress.

An ADI should have a full understanding of the risk profile of hardship loans and ensure these risks are appropriately reflected in internal management reporting, provisioning and capital adequacy calculations.

Where a residential mortgage loan is in default, a prudent ADI should usually undertake a full revaluation when assessing the value of the collateral, with alternative valuations only appropriate in limited circumstances such as very low LVR.

For prudential reporting purposes, ADIs should not freeze or re-age loans where hardship concessions have been granted; arrears should continue to accrue based on original scheduled payments until the loan returns to performing status.

Hardship or collections decisions involving ongoing concessions beyond normally available terms (non-commercial terms) must be reported as 'restructured items' in APRA prudential returns, and appropriate loan-loss collective provisioning should be maintained for loans subject to hardship applications.

A prudent ADI should regularly stress test its residential mortgage lending portfolio under a range of severe but plausible adverse scenarios, with complexity and granularity reflecting the scale and risk characteristics of the portfolio.
Stress test models should be appropriately validated and checked by an independent internal group or qualified external party; vendor models should be customised to the ADI's specific risk profile.

Stress testing arrangements should include well-documented policies and procedures, with timely communication of results to the Board and senior management including quantitative information on scenarios, results, capital impact, key assumptions and recommended actions.

LMI is not an alternative to loan origination due diligence; a prudent ADI should, notwithstanding LMI coverage, conduct its own comprehensive and independent assessment of borrower repayment capacity, minimum initial equity, debt service coverage and property value.

An ADI must ensure compliance with all LMI provider requirements to avoid invalidating cover; LMI requirements and ADI obligations should be reflected in the residential mortgage lending risk management framework, with internal audit and/or risk management functions periodically testing compliance.
APRA expects an ADI to have its own hindsight review and audit regime rather than relying solely on the audit and compliance regimes of LMI providers.
